draft.introduction: Ennead 2.9, “Against the Gnostics,” is Plotinus’s polemical refutation of Gnostic cosmology. He attacks their contempt for the material world, their multiplication of divine beings, and their claim to special knowledge unavailable through philosophical inquiry.

translation.text: What is the use of this parade of terrors? If they mean to teach philosophy, they should set aside these theatrical frights and say plainly and fairly what they have to say about the nature of things, since they are addressing those who seek the truth, not trying to create new myths.

Those who despise what is akin to the divine nature show that they know nothing of the divine nature itself. For he who truly loves a person loves everything connected with that person. And the offspring of the Father are beautiful: the sun and the stars and this entire universe. What man of sense could call this cosmos ugly, when it proceeds from the beauty of the intelligible world?
translation.original_text: οἱ δὲ τὸ συγγενὲς τῇ θείᾳ φύσει ἀτιμάζοντες

translator_notes.note: Plotinus’s defense of the material cosmos against Gnostic contempt profoundly influenced Christian theology. Augustine, a former Manichean, found in Plotinus the philosophical resources to affirm creation’s goodness. The argument that despising the visible world reveals ignorance of the invisible remains a cornerstone of sacramental theology.
